summary: Gruber presents the Tennessee school board's banning of Art Spiegelman's 'Maus' as a three-act story of censorship backfiring. Act 1 covers the McMinn County board's unanimous vote to remove the Pulitzer-winning Holocaust graphic novel from eighth-grade curriculum over cursing and nudity. Act 2 invokes Stephen King's 1996 speech arguing that censorship is about control, not family values, and urging kids to seek out banned books. Act 3 delivers the punchline: 'Maus' shot to the top of Amazon's bestseller lists after the ban. The structure itself is the argument — censorship reliably produces the Streisand effect.
key_points: A Tennessee school board unanimously banned 'Maus' from eighth-grade classrooms over cursing and a nude depiction, despite the book being a Pulitzer Prize-winning Holocaust narrative
Stephen King's 1996 speech frames censorship as intellectual arrogance and control — the impulse to decide what's bad for everyone's family based on one's own discomfort
King's advice to young people: run to the nearest library or bookstore and read exactly what they tried to keep from you
The ban backfired spectacularly, sending multiple editions of 'Maus' to the top of Amazon's bestseller lists
money_quotes: Censorship and the suppression of reading materials is rarely about family values and almost always about control.
If it's bad for me and my family, it's bad for everyone's family.

Don't spend time waving signs or carrying petitions around the neighborhood. Instead run — don't walk — to the nearest non-school library or to the local bookstore, and get whatever it was they banned.
Read what they're trying to keep out of your eyes. Read what they're trying to keep out of your brain. Because that's exactly what you need to know.
This bit of intellectual arrogance — undemocratic and as old as time.
key_insight: Gruber lets the three-act structure speak for itself: censorship is not only morally wrong but practically self-defeating, as banning 'Maus' turned it into a bestseller.
